what_it_does: A drift report produced by _system/refcheck.py that checks every code: reference declared in register.json (features[].code) and addresses.json (addresses.*.code) against the current ~/company snapshot, recording where each reference lands (points_at), the symbol hint expected from its owner label (candidates), and whether a repair is needed.
observations: Validates 215 references drawn from two registries: register.json and addresses.json.
Each entry carries an owner label, a represents description, candidate symbols, a resolved file path, and a points_at landing.
Many references resolve to a different symbol than the one named in the ref or expected by the owner label.
Some references only resolve to a file with no line or no named symbol.
The report explicitly distinguishes code refs in features[].code from feature-id references in views[].represents.
repair_target is null for every visible entry; the shown portion contains only ok[] entries, while drift[] is not visible due to truncation.
Addresses entries use ui:// owners and often have empty candidates lists.
Snapshot is pinned at commit 2d704fdd7562b5c45c5621b3e5924167f283412a on 2026-06-09 15:40:04 +1000.
standouts: A large share of entries show symbol-name mismatches between the declared reference and the actual landing symbol.
The report is framed as a repair worklist input for a lead who fixes registries centrally rather than editing code.
It cross-links backend Python files with frontend TSX/CSS files in a single ledger.
Many runtime/suite.py references are coarse or land on adjacent/internal helpers instead of the named public method.
conventions: JSON schema with _what metadata, snapshot, counts, and ok/drift arrays.
Owner labels use prefixes such as ENG-, NODE-, CAN-, RUN-, RHM-, WALK-, INB-, WIRE-, SM-, MOD-, GOV-, EVT-, WS-, INTRO-, FRM-, and ui://.
References are encoded as file:symbol or file:line.
Candidate symbols are derived from the owner label and represents description.
points_at phrasing varies: 'def @ line X, +Y', 'file ... exists (no line given)', or 'defines <symbol> @ line X'.
concerns: Many ok[] entries show repair_target null despite clear symbol mismatches, so ok[] may mean 'resolved somewhere' rather than 'correct'.
Heavy reliance on line numbers that will drift with every edit; the snapshot note warns to re-run after registry repairs.
Some owner labels are overloaded, e.g., runtime/suite.py:chat is used for both RHM-chat and RHM-decide.
ui:// address entries often lack candidate symbols, making automatic drift detection harder.
The file is truncated, so the actual drift[] worklist is not visible.
notes: A registry-hygiene audit listing where each design reference currently lands, with mismatches intended to be repaired centrally in register.json and addresses.json.
questions: What criteria move an entry from ok[] to drift[]?
Is repair_target null because no repair is needed, or because the field is only populated in drift[]?
How are candidates generated from owner labels, and what threshold counts as a mismatch?
Are line-number-only refs expected to point at a specific symbol name or just any definition at that line?
What is the full shape of the drift[] array and its repair_target schema?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to produce drift findings for code refs; the visible content does that for ok[] entries, but the actual drift worklist (drift[]) is not shown in the truncated file, so the shown portion reads more as a resolution map than a visible repair list.
intention_signals: It is trying to prevent design documentation in register.json and addresses.json from silently pointing at the wrong code as the codebase evolves, by automating a periodic reference check and producing a human repair worklist.
novelty: A dedicated reference-drift report that treats design registry entries as first-class references to be validated against source, separate from static code analysis or runtime call graphs.
contribution: It contributes a machine-readable, snapshot-pinned map of every declared code/design reference and its current landing location, enabling registry repair workflows and cross-stack traceability.
summary_for_embedding: A JSON drift report that checks code references in register.json and addresses.json against a ~/company snapshot, recording expected symbols, actual landing points, and repair status for 215 references.
intention_for_embedding: To surface where design registry references no longer point at their intended code symbols so a lead can repair the registries centrally.
